title: Quiz: Symmetry-Preserving Ansatze
description: Test your understanding of symmetry exploitation in variational quantum algorithms

questions.question: What does it mean for an operator S to be a symmetry of Hamiltonian H?
questions.explanation: A symmetry operator S commutes with the Hamiltonian: [H, S] = HS - SH = 0. This means eigenstates of H can also be eigenstates of S, and different symmetry sectors don't mix.

questions.question: Why is particle number conservation important for molecular simulations?

options.text: Molecules have fixed electron count and valid states must preserve it

questions.explanation: Molecules have a fixed number of electrons. A valid wavefunction must preserve this number. Ansatze that break particle number explore unphysical states, wasting computational resources.

questions.question: What type of gate preserves particle number in fermionic systems?
questions.explanation: Fermionic excitation operators a†ₚaᵧ move particles between orbitals without changing the total count. Standard gates like Ry or CNOT can create or destroy particles in the computational basis.

questions.question: What does spin-adapted mean in the context of molecular ansatze?

options.text: The ansatz can change spin quantum numbers
Alpha and beta electrons are treated together to preserve S² and Sᵧ

questions.explanation: Spin-adapted ansatze couple alpha and beta electron excitations to maintain total spin quantum numbers (S² and Sᵧ). For singlets, this means pairing alpha and beta excitations.

questions.question: Hardware-efficient ansatze typically preserve all physical symmetries automatically.
questions.explanation: False. Standard hardware-efficient ansatze (like layers of Ry/Rz and CNOT) typically break all physical symmetries including particle number and spin, exploring unphysical regions of Hilbert space.

questions.question: What is qubit tapering?

options.text: Using Z₂ symmetries to reduce the number of qubits needed

questions.explanation: Qubit tapering uses Z₂ symmetries (operators that square to identity and commute with H) to eliminate qubits. Each Z₂ symmetry allows removing one qubit while staying in the desired symmetry sector.

questions.question: How do point group symmetries (like C2v) help in molecular VQE?

options.text: They restrict which excitations are allowed, reducing parameters
They are only relevant for classical calculations

questions.explanation: Point group symmetry determines which orbital-to-orbital excitations are symmetry-allowed. Only excitations whose direct product contains the target state's irrep are included, significantly reducing the parameter count.

questions.question: What are benefits of symmetry-preserving ansatze? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Symmetry-preserving ansatze (a) have fewer parameters (only physical excitations), (b) converge faster (confined to relevant subspace), and (d) avoid unphysical solutions. However, they don't eliminate quantum noise—though symmetry violations can help detect noise.
